meta.name: Build Your Own TCP/IP Stack
meta.description: Implement a userspace TCP/IP network stack from scratch, handling Ethernet frame parsing, ARP resolution, IP routing, ICMP, and a full TCP implementation with connection management, reliable delivery, and flow/congestion control.
meta.essence: Byte-level protocol parsing with correct endianness handling, state machine management for TCP connection lifecycle, and implementing reliable ordered delivery over unreliable packet networks through sequence numbers, acknowledgments, retransmission timers, sliding window flow control, and congestion control algorithms.
meta.why_important: Building a TCP/IP stack from scratch teaches fundamental network programming concepts that underpin all modern distributed systems, from databases to microservices, and provides deep insight into how operating systems manage network communication at the kernel level.
learning_outcomes: Implement Ethernet frame parsing and ARP protocol for hardware address resolution
Design and implement IP packet routing with ICMP echo request/reply (ping)
Handle IP fragmentation and reassembly for packets exceeding MTU
Build TCP connection establishment using 3-way handshake and full state machine transitions including TIME_WAIT
Implement TCP checksum calculation with pseudo-header covering IP fields
Negotiate TCP options including Maximum Segment Size (MSS) during connection setup
Implement sliding window flow control with sequence numbers and acknowledgments
Handle out-of-order segment buffering and reordering
Implement retransmission with adaptive RTO calculation (Jacobson/Karn's algorithm)
Build congestion control with slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit, and fast recovery
Debug low-level network protocols using packet capture and analysis tools
Manage concurrent connection state across multiple TCP sessions

prerequisites.name: Networking fundamentals (OSI model, basic socket programming)
C or Rust programming (pointer arithmetic, memory management)

milestones.name: Ethernet & ARP
milestones.description: Set up raw packet I/O via TAP device or raw socket, parse Ethernet frames, and implement ARP request/reply for IP-to-MAC address resolution.
acceptance_criteria: Raw socket or TAP device successfully receives and sends Ethernet frames on the network interface
Ethernet parser correctly extracts 6-byte source MAC, 6-byte destination MAC, and 2-byte EtherType field
Parser correctly handles IEEE 802.1Q VLAN-tagged frames by detecting EtherType 0x8100 and skipping the 4-byte tag
ARP reply with correct hardware and protocol addresses is sent when an ARP request matches our configured IP
ARP table caches resolved entries and evicts stale entries after a configurable timeout (default 60 seconds)
ARP request is sent for unknown destination IPs, and outbound packets are queued until resolution completes or times out
All multi-byte fields are converted between network byte order (big-endian) and host byte order using explicit conversion functions
pitfalls: Byte order: all Ethernet/ARP fields are big-endian on the wire; forgetting ntohs/ntohl causes silent corruption
ARP cache poisoning is trivial — do not trust unsolicited ARP replies in production, but accept them for this project scope
Broadcast frames (destination FF: FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) must be handled; dropping them breaks ARP
TAP device MTU defaults may differ from physical interface MTU, causing silent truncation
Forgetting to set the correct EtherType (0x0806 for ARP, 0x0800 for IPv4) causes frames to be dropped by receivers
deliverables: Raw socket or TAP device setup for capturing and injecting network frames
Ethernet frame parser extracting source MAC, destination MAC, EtherType, and payload
ARP request/reply handler resolving IP addresses to MAC addresses per RFC 826
ARP cache with configurable TTL expiration and pending-request queue for outbound packets

milestones.name: IP Layer & ICMP
milestones.description: Implement IPv4 packet parsing, header checksum calculation, IP fragmentation reassembly, basic routing table lookup, and ICMP echo request/reply (ping).
concepts: Routing table and longest prefix match
acceptance_criteria: IPv4 parser correctly extracts version, IHL, total length, identification, flags, fragment offset, TTL, protocol, source IP, and destination IP
IP header checksum is computed using RFC 1071 ones' complement sum and verified on every received packet; packets with invalid checksums are dropped silently
ICMP echo reply is sent with correct identifier and sequence number in response to incoming echo request (ping responds within 10ms on local network)
IP fragmentation reassembly correctly reconstructs original datagram from fragments using identification field and fragment offset; incomplete fragments are discarded after a 30-second timeout
Routing table supports static route entries and selects the correct next-hop gateway using longest prefix match
TTL is decremented on forwarded packets; packets with TTL=0 are dropped and ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11) is sent back to source
Packets destined for our own IP addresses are delivered to the upper-layer protocol handler (TCP/ICMP)
pitfalls: IP header checksum covers only the header, not the payload — a common mistake is checksumming the entire packet
IHL (Internet Header Length) is in 4-byte words, not bytes; forgetting to multiply by 4 causes incorrect payload offset
Fragment reassembly must handle overlapping fragments and out-of-order arrival; a simple array indexed by offset works
TTL must be decremented before forwarding, not after; decrementing after forwarding violates RFC 791
ICMP checksum is separate from IP checksum and covers the entire ICMP message including data
deliverables: IPv4 packet parser and builder with full header field extraction and construction
IP header checksum calculator using RFC 1071 ones' complement algorithm
IP fragmentation reassembly module with timeout-based cleanup of incomplete datagrams
ICMP echo request/reply handler implementing ping functionality
Static routing table with longest prefix match lookup and next-hop selection

milestones.name: TCP Connection Management
milestones.description: Implement TCP segment parsing with checksum verification using the IP pseudo-header, the full TCP state machine including 3-way handshake, MSS option negotiation, and graceful connection teardown with TIME_WAIT.
acceptance_criteria: TCP segment parser extracts source port, destination port, sequence number, acknowledgment number, data offset, flags (SYN/ACK/FIN/RST/PSH), window size, checksum, and urgent pointer
TCP checksum is computed over the pseudo-header (source IP, destination IP, zero byte, protocol 6, TCP length) plus the TCP segment; segments with invalid checksums are dropped
3-way handshake completes successfully — server transitions LISTEN→SYN_RECEIVED→ESTABLISHED; client transitions CLOSED→SYN_SENT→ESTABLISHED
MSS option is parsed from SYN/SYN-ACK segments and used to limit outbound segment payload size; default MSS of 536 bytes is used when option is absent
State machine implements all 11 TCP states from RFC 793 with correct transitions, including simultaneous open and simultaneous close
RST segments are handled correctly — received RST aborts the connection; RST is sent in response to segments received in invalid states
4-way FIN handshake gracefully closes connection; the closing side enters TIME_WAIT for 2*MSL (default 60 seconds) to absorb delayed segments
Connection table supports multiple concurrent connections keyed by (local IP, local port, remote IP, remote port) tuple
pitfalls: TCP checksum pseudo-header is the most error-prone part — it requires fields from the IP layer, breaking clean layer separation
Sequence number arithmetic must use modulo 2^32 comparison (e.g., SEQ_LT, SEQ_GT macros) because sequence numbers wrap around
Forgetting TIME_WAIT causes connection reuse to accept stale segments from previous connections
Simultaneous open (both sides send SYN) is rare but must be handled or the state machine will deadlock
RST handling is asymmetric — receiving RST in SYN_RECEIVED returns to LISTEN, but in ESTABLISHED it aborts
ISN (Initial Sequence Number) should not be zero or predictable; use a time-based or random generator per RFC 6528
deliverables: TCP segment parser and builder with full header field extraction including options
TCP checksum calculator using IP pseudo-header per RFC 793
MSS option negotiation during SYN/SYN-ACK exchange
Full TCP state machine implementing all 11 states with transition validation
Connection teardown with FIN handshake and TIME_WAIT timer
Connection table managing concurrent sessions by 4-tuple

milestones.name: TCP Reliable Delivery & Flow Control
milestones.description: Implement sliding window protocol for flow control, out-of-order segment buffering, retransmission with adaptive RTO, and basic congestion control (slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit/fast recovery).
concepts: Congestion window (cwnd) and slow start threshold (ssthresh)
acceptance_criteria: Sender sliding window allows sending up to min(cwnd, rwnd) bytes of unacknowledged data before blocking
Receiver advertised window (rwnd) is respected; sender never has more unacknowledged bytes than the receiver's advertised window
Out-of-order segments are buffered at the receiver and delivered to the application in-order once gaps are filled
RTO is calculated using Jacobson's algorithm (SRTT, RTTVAR) per RFC 6298; initial RTO is 1 second, minimum is 1 second
Karn's algorithm is applied — RTT samples are not taken from retransmitted segments to avoid ambiguity
Unacknowledged segments are retransmitted when RTO expires; RTO is doubled (exponential backoff) on each successive timeout for the same segment
Slow start increases cwnd by 1 MSS per ACK received (exponential growth) until cwnd reaches ssthresh
Congestion avoidance increases cwnd by MSS*(MSS/cwnd) per ACK (approximately 1 MSS per RTT) when cwnd >= ssthresh
Fast retransmit triggers on receipt of 3 duplicate ACKs, retransmitting the missing segment without waiting for RTO
Fast recovery sets ssthresh = cwnd/2 and cwnd = ssthresh + 3*MSS after fast retransmit, then inflates cwnd by 1 MSS per additional duplicate ACK
End-to-end test transfers a 1MB file over the stack and verifies byte-for-byte correctness with SHA-256 comparison
pitfalls: Timer management is the #1 source of bugs — use a single timer wheel or min-heap, not per-segment timers
Window calculation must use modulo 2^32 arithmetic; naive subtraction overflows on sequence wraparound
Nagle's algorithm (RFC 896) can interact badly with delayed ACKs causing 200ms delays; consider implementing or explicitly disabling
Zero-window probing is needed when receiver advertises rwnd=0; without it, the connection deadlocks
Silly window syndrome occurs when receiver advertises tiny windows; Clark's algorithm or sender-side Nagle mitigates this
Fast retransmit/recovery must not be triggered during slow start or when there's insufficient duplicate ACK data
deliverables: Sender sliding window tracking bytes in flight against min(cwnd, rwnd)
Receiver-side out-of-order segment buffer with gap tracking and in-order delivery
Adaptive RTO calculator implementing Jacobson's algorithm with Karn's amendment
Retransmission timer with exponential backoff on repeated timeouts
Congestion control module implementing slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit, and fast recovery per RFC 5681
Zero-window probe mechanism preventing deadlock when receiver window closes
End-to-end file transfer test verifying reliable, ordered delivery

blueprint.title: Build Your Own TCP/IP Stack
blueprint.overview: This project implements a complete userspace TCP/IP network stack from the ground up, transforming raw Ethernet frames into reliable, ordered byte streams. You'll build every layer: Ethernet frame parsing with VLAN handling, ARP for MAC resolution, IPv4 with fragmentation reassembly, ICMP for diagnostics, and a full TCP implementation featuring the 3-way handshake, sliding window flow control, adaptive retransmission timers, and congestion control algorithms (slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit/recovery). The stack runs on Linux using TAP devices or raw sockets, giving you direct access to network frames without kernel modifications.
blueprint.design_philosophy: TCP/IP is often taught as abstract layers with clean separations, but the reality is messier and more interconnected. The TCP checksum requires IP-layer fields (pseudo-header), sequence numbers wrap around requiring modular arithmetic, and congestion control depends on timing data from ACKs. By building each layer yourself, you'll understand why these cross-layer dependencies exist and how 40 years of Internet evolution shaped the protocols. The core challenge is managing concurrent state across multiple dimensions: connection state machines, retransmission timers, receive buffers for out-of-order segments, and congestion window dynamics—all while handling the fundamental unreliability of IP packets.

implementation.rationale: TCP/IP stack implementation demands direct memory manipulation for packet parsing, explicit endianness control for network byte order conversion, and manual buffer management for zero-copy operations. C provides ntohs()/ntohl()/htons()/htonl() as standard primitives, direct struct overlays for packet headers, and the ability to reason precisely about every byte on the wire. Rust would add safety but complicate the learning with borrow checker friction around buffer lifetimes; Go's GC would make buffer pooling patterns unnatural.
implementation.style_guide: snake_case for functions and variables; UPPER_CASE for constants and macros; struct names in snake_case_t; all multi-byte network fields explicitly converted with ntoh*/hton* functions (never rely on struct packing); use static inline functions for hot paths; prefix all module functions with module name (arp_, ip_, tcp_, eth_); use uint8_t/uint16_t/uint32_t consistently from stdint.h
implementation.build_system: Makefile with separate compilation units per protocol layer (eth.o, arp.o, ip.o, icmp.o, tcp.o), debug build with -g -O0 -fsanitize=address, release with -O2 -DNDEBUG, and a 'test' target that runs the end-to-end file transfer
alternatives: Rust: Excellent for production stacks (used in smoltcp, netstack-smoltcp) with memory safety guarantees, but borrow checker adds friction when learning buffer management patterns
Go: Good for understanding protocols without memory management distraction, but GC makes zero-copy patterns impossible and struct packing is less predictable

assumed_known: C pointers and memory management (malloc/free, pointer arithmetic)
Basic networking concepts (IP addresses, ports, OSI layer model)
Socket programming basics (at least client-side connect/send/recv)
Finite state machine fundamentals (states, transitions, events)

must_teach_first.why: Every multi-byte field in Ethernet/ARP/IP/TCP is big-endian on the wire; x86 is little-endian; this mismatch causes subtle bugs if not internalized
The TCP checksum is computed over a synthetic header containing IP addresses—this cross-layer dependency breaks clean abstractions and is the #1 source of TCP implementation bugs
Sequence numbers wrap around; naive comparisons fail after 4GB transferred; SEQ_LT/SEQ_GT macros are mandatory
Per-segment timers are O(n) and don't scale; understanding why a single timer data structure is necessary prevents architectural mistakes

milestones.title: Ethernet & ARP
milestones.summary: Establish raw packet I/O through TAP device or raw socket, parse Ethernet frames including VLAN-tagged variants, and implement ARP for dynamic IP-to-MAC address resolution with caching.
milestones.misconception: Developers often think Ethernet is just 'send bytes to a MAC address'—a simple delivery service. They assume the OS handles all the framing details and that IP addresses map directly to physical devices.
milestones.reveal: Ethernet is a shared-medium broadcast protocol at its core. Your frame goes to EVERY device on the segment; the destination MAC is just a filter. ARP is a trustless, stateless protocol where any device can claim any IP-to-MAC mapping—there's no authentication, no verification, and the cache can be poisoned trivially. The 'simple' act of sending an IP packet requires first broadcasting 'WHO HAS THIS IP?' and waiting for a response before you even know where to send.
cascade: DHCP (cross-domain): The same broadcast mechanism that ARP uses is how DHCP works—your device broadcasts 'I need an IP' and any DHCP server can respond
Switch MAC Learning: Network switches build their forwarding tables by watching ARP traffic and source MAC addresses—understanding ARP explains why switches 'learn' topology
VLANs and Network Isolation: Those 802.1Q tags you're parsing? They're how datacenters isolate thousands of tenants on shared physical infrastructure
IPv6 Neighbor Discovery: IPv6 doesn't use ARP—it uses ICMPv6 for the same purpose, but the conceptual model is identical once you understand Layer 2 resolution
Container Networking (cross-domain): Docker/Kubernetes overlay networks are just fancy ARP + VLAN + encapsulation—your mental model scales to cloud-native networking
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Raw socket or TAP device successfully receives and sends Ethernet frames on the network interface
Ethernet parser correctly extracts 6-byte source MAC, 6-byte destination MAC, and 2-byte EtherType field
Parser correctly handles IEEE 802.1Q VLAN-tagged frames by detecting EtherType 0x8100 and skipping the 4-byte tag
ARP reply with correct hardware and protocol addresses is sent when an ARP request matches our configured IP
ARP table caches resolved entries and evicts stale entries after a configurable timeout (default 60 seconds)
ARP request is sent for unknown destination IPs, and outbound packets are queued until resolution completes or times out
All multi-byte fields are converted between network byte order (big-endian) and host byte order using explicit conversion functions

milestones.title: IP Layer & ICMP
milestones.summary: Implement IPv4 packet parsing with header checksum verification, IP fragmentation reassembly, static routing with longest-prefix-match, and ICMP echo request/reply for network diagnostics.
milestones.misconception: Developers often think IP is 'just the address part' of networking—a simple envelope around TCP. They assume packets arrive in order, fragmentation is rare, and routing is just 'find the destination.'
milestones.reveal: IP is a best-effort, connectionless protocol that makes NO guarantees. Packets can be duplicated, lost, reordered, or fragmented mid-flight. A single 1500-byte TCP segment might arrive as three 500-byte fragments from three different routers—and you must reassemble them. The 'routing' you think of is actually 'next-hop selection' based on longest prefix match, and the TTL field is a countdown timer that kills packets before they can loop forever.
cascade: Path MTU Discovery: The reason fragmentation exists is because different links have different MTUs—understanding fragmentation is prerequisite to understanding why MTU discovery matters for TCP performance
traceroute (cross-domain): This ubiquitous debugging tool works by exploiting TTL expiration—send packets with TTL=1,2,3... and collect the ICMP Time Exceeded responses to map the path
IPsec and VPNs: VPNs encapsulate IP in IP (or UDP)—your IP parsing code is the foundation for understanding tunnel protocols

Anycast and CDN Routing (cross-domain): When you ping a CDN IP, you're not pinging one server—you're hitting the 'nearest' server advertising that prefix. Longest-prefix-match + BGP = global load balancing
NAT Traversal: NAT rewrites IP addresses and ports—understanding IP header structure is mandatory for understanding why NAT breaks end-to-end connectivity
yaml_acceptance_criteria: IPv4 parser correctly extracts version, IHL, total length, identification, flags, fragment offset, TTL, protocol, source IP, and destination IP
IP header checksum is computed using RFC 1071 ones' complement sum and verified on every received packet; packets with invalid checksums are dropped silently
ICMP echo reply is sent with correct identifier and sequence number in response to incoming echo request (ping responds within 10ms on local network)
IP fragmentation reassembly correctly reconstructs original datagram from fragments using identification field and fragment offset; incomplete fragments are discarded after a 30-second timeout
Routing table supports static route entries and selects the correct next-hop gateway using longest prefix match
TTL is decremented on forwarded packets; packets with TTL=0 are dropped and ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11) is sent back to source
Packets destined for our own IP addresses are delivered to the upper-layer protocol handler (TCP/ICMP)

milestones.title: TCP Connection Management
milestones.summary: Implement TCP segment parsing with pseudo-header checksum verification, the complete 11-state TCP state machine including 3-way handshake and 4-way teardown, MSS option negotiation, and connection table for concurrent sessions.
milestones.misconception: Developers think TCP connections are 'open, send, receive, close'—a simple pipe. They assume the kernel handles all the complexity and that connect() is instantaneous.
milestones.reveal: A TCP connection is a distributed agreement between two independent machines about 64 bits of state (sequence numbers) plus window sizes. The 'simple' connect() involves 3 packets traveling over an unreliable network where any could be lost, duplicated, or delayed by minutes. The 3-way handshake exists because of the Two Generals Problem—it's theoretically impossible to guarantee both sides agree the connection exists. TIME_WAIT lasts 2 minutes because a delayed packet from a previous connection could corrupt a new one using the same port pair.
cascade: TLS Handshake (cross-domain): HTTPS adds another handshake on top of TCP—understanding TCP's 3-way handshake makes TLS's RTT costs (1-RTT vs 0-RTT) intuitive
HTTP Keep-Alive and Connection Pooling: Why does HTTP/1.1 reuse connections? Because TCP handshake costs 1.5 RTTs minimum—understanding the handshake explains application-layer optimization
Load Balancer Health Checks: Those 'TCP connection succeeded' health checks? They're completing 3-way handshakes—understanding states explains why some health checks are 'half-open'
SYN Flood Attacks (cross-domain security): DoS attacks exploit the state machine by sending SYNs without completing—understanding SYN_RECEIVED state explains SYN cookies and backlog queues
QUIC (cross-domain): Google's UDP-based protocol replaces TCP's handshake with its own—understanding WHY TCP needs 3 packets helps you understand what QUIC optimizes
yaml_acceptance_criteria: TCP segment parser extracts source port, destination port, sequence number, acknowledgment number, data offset, flags (SYN/ACK/FIN/RST/PSH), window size, checksum, and urgent pointer
TCP checksum is computed over the pseudo-header (source IP, destination IP, zero byte, protocol 6, TCP length) plus the TCP segment; segments with invalid checksums are dropped
3-way handshake completes successfully — server transitions LISTEN→SYN_RECEIVED→ESTABLISHED; client transitions CLOSED→SYN_SENT→ESTABLISHED
MSS option is parsed from SYN/SYN-ACK segments and used to limit outbound segment payload size; default MSS of 536 bytes is used when option is absent
State machine implements all 11 TCP states from RFC 793 with correct transitions, including simultaneous open and simultaneous close
RST segments are handled correctly — received RST aborts the connection; RST is sent in response to segments received in invalid states
4-way FIN handshake gracefully closes connection; the closing side enters TIME_WAIT for 2*MSL (default 60 seconds) to absorb delayed segments
Connection table supports multiple concurrent connections keyed by (local IP, local port, remote IP, remote port) tuple

milestones.title: TCP Reliable Delivery & Flow Control
milestones.summary: Implement sliding window protocol with sender/receiver flow control, out-of-order segment buffering, adaptive retransmission timeout using Jacobson's algorithm with Karn's amendment, and complete congestion control (slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit, fast recovery).
milestones.misconception: Developers think TCP reliability means 'send data, get ACK, done'—a simple request-response pattern. They assume if the network is fast, TCP is fast, and congestion is someone else's problem.
milestones.reveal: TCP reliability is a continuous negotiation between sender, receiver, and the network itself. The 'window' isn't a fixed buffer—it's dynamically computed from min(congestion_window, receiver_window). The congestion window starts at 1 MSS and grows exponentially (slow start) until the network signals distress (packet loss), then backs off linearly. A 'simple' 1MB file transfer might trigger dozens of retransmissions, multiple congestion window reductions, and involve buffering out-of-order segments—all invisible to the application. The RTO isn't fixed; it's continuously recomputed from measured RTT variance using floating-point math derived from 1988 research.
cascade: HTTP/2 Multiplexing (cross-domain): Why does HTTP/2 multiplex streams over one TCP connection? Because TCP congestion control is per-connection—understanding cwnd explains why connection reuse matters
BBR Congestion Control: Google's BBR replaces loss-based congestion control with delay-based—understanding 'traditional' congestion control is prerequisite to understanding the evolution
Bufferbloat (cross-domain): Those large buffers in home routers interact badly with TCP congestion control—understanding cwnd explains why 'more buffer' can mean 'worse latency'
TCP Tuning for Datacenters (cross-domain): High-performance systems tune initcwnd, disable Nagle, adjust buffers—understanding the algorithms explains which knobs matter
QUIC's Loss Recovery (cross-domain): QUIC implements TCP-style congestion control in userspace—your implementation concepts directly transfer to the 'future of TCP'
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Sender sliding window allows sending up to min(cwnd, rwnd) bytes of unacknowledged data before blocking
Receiver advertised window (rwnd) is respected; sender never has more unacknowledged bytes than the receiver's advertised window
Out-of-order segments are buffered at the receiver and delivered to the application in-order once gaps are filled
RTO is calculated using Jacobson's algorithm (SRTT, RTTVAR) per RFC 6298; initial RTO is 1 second, minimum is 1 second
Karn's algorithm is applied — RTT samples are not taken from retransmitted segments to avoid ambiguity
Unacknowledged segments are retransmitted when RTO expires; RTO is doubled (exponential backoff) on each successive timeout for the same segment
Slow start increases cwnd by 1 MSS per ACK received (exponential growth) until cwnd reaches ssthresh
Congestion avoidance increases cwnd by MSS*(MSS/cwnd) per ACK (approximately 1 MSS per RTT) when cwnd >= ssthresh
Fast retransmit triggers on receipt of 3 duplicate ACKs, retransmitting the missing segment without waiting for RTO
Fast recovery sets ssthresh = cwnd/2 and cwnd = ssthresh + 3*MSS after fast retransmit, then inflates cwnd by 1 MSS per additional duplicate ACK
End-to-end test transfers a 1MB file over the stack and verifies byte-for-byte correctness with SHA-256 comparison

diagrams.title: TCP/IP Stack Architecture: Satellite View
diagrams.description: The complete stack from application to wire, showing all four milestones as interconnected layers. Each layer shows its primary responsibility and the anchor IDs that link to detailed views. Application at top, Ethernet/TAP at bottom, with data flowing down on send and up on receive.

diagrams.title: Ethernet Frame Byte Layout
diagrams.description: Memory layout of an Ethernet frame showing byte offsets: Preamble (7B), SFD (1B), Destination MAC (6B), Source MAC (6B), 802.1Q Tag optional (4B), EtherType (2B), Payload (46-1500B), FCS (4B). Shows both standard and VLAN-tagged frames side by side with byte positions.

diagrams.title: Ethernet Frame Parsing State Machine
diagrams.description: State machine for parsing Ethernet frames: WAIT_PREAMBLE → WAIT_SFD → READ_DST_MAC → READ_SRC_MAC → CHECK_ETHERTYPE → [if 0x8100] SKIP_VLAN_TAG → READ_PAYLOAD → VERIFY_FCS. Shows error states for invalid frames.

diagrams.title: ARP Request/Reply Message Flow
diagrams.description: Timeline showing ARP resolution: Host A broadcasts 'WHO HAS 192.168.1.100?' (dst MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF), Host B responds with unicast '192.168.1.100 IS AT aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff'. Shows packet contents at each step including hardware type, protocol type, and addresses.

diagrams.title: ARP Cache Data Structure
diagrams.description: Hash table structure for ARP cache: IP address as key, (MAC address, timestamp, state) as value. States: RESOLVED, PENDING, STALE. Shows pending packet queue attached to PENDING entries. Timer sweeps entries older than TTL.

diagrams.title: Network vs Host Byte Order Conversion
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of 0x12345678 in memory: big-endian (network order) [12 34 56 78] vs little-endian (x86) [78 56 34 12]. Shows ntohs/ntohl/htons/htonl conversion flow with 16-bit and 32-bit examples.

diagrams.title: TAP Device Architecture
diagrams.description: How TAP device connects userspace stack to kernel network stack: Application → /dev/net/tun → TAP interface (tap0) → Kernel bridge/routing → Physical interface. Shows packet path for both directions with file descriptor I/O.

diagrams.title: Ethernet Send/Receive Data Path
diagrams.description: Dual-path diagram showing: (1) Receive path: poll() → read() from TAP fd → parse Ethernet → dispatch by EtherType → ARP/IP handlers. (2) Send path: upper layer → Ethernet header assembly → write() to TAP fd. Shows buffer ownership at each stage.

diagrams.title: IPv4 Header Byte Layout
diagrams.description: Complete IPv4 header with byte offsets and bit positions: Version (4b), IHL (4b), DSCP (6b), ECN (2b), Total Length (16b), Identification (16b), Flags (3b), Fragment Offset (13b), TTL (8b), Protocol (8b), Header Checksum (16b), Source IP (32b), Destination IP (32b), Options (variable). Shows 20-byte minimum header.

diagrams.title: IP Checksum Calculation (RFC 1071)
diagrams.description: Step-by-step checksum computation: (1) Treat header as 16-bit words, (2) Sum all words using ones' complement addition (carry wraps around), (3) Take ones' complement of final sum. Shows example with actual header bytes and intermediate sums.

diagrams.title: IP Fragmentation and Reassembly
diagrams.description: Shows 4000-byte datagram fragmented across two 1500-byte MTU links: Original → [Fragment 1: offset=0, MF=1, 1500B] + [Fragment 2: offset=1480, MF=1, 1500B] + [Fragment 3: offset=2960, MF=0, 1020B]. Reassembly buffer shows gap-filling as fragments arrive out of order.

diagrams.title: IP Fragment Reassembly Buffer Structure
diagrams.description: Data structure for reassembly: hash table keyed by (Source IP, Dest IP, Identification), each entry contains: total length expected, timer, bitmap of received fragments, data buffer with holes. Shows hole-filling algorithm.

diagrams.title: Routing Table with Longest Prefix Match
diagrams.description: Trie/prefix tree structure for routing lookup: root → branches for each bit of destination IP. Shows example with routes 10.0.0.0/8, 10.1.0.0/16, 192.168.1.0/24, default 0.0.0.0/0. Demonstrates how lookup for 10.1.2.3 matches /16 not /8.

diagrams.title: ICMP Echo Request/Reply (Ping) Flow
diagrams.description: Timeline of ping exchange: Echo Request (Type 8, Code 0, ID, Seq, Data) → IP router → Echo Reply (Type 0, Code 0, same ID/Seq/Data). Shows RTT calculation and how traceroute exploits TTL for path discovery.

diagrams.title: IP Layer Receive Processing Path
diagrams.description: Flowchart: Receive packet → Verify IP header checksum → Check destination IP (ours?) → Decrement TTL → Parse options → [if fragmented] Queue for reassembly → Dispatch by protocol field (1=ICMP, 6=TCP, 17=UDP) → Deliver to upper layer. Shows drop points for invalid packets.

diagrams.title: IP Layer Send Processing Path
diagrams.description: Flowchart: Upper layer provides payload → Set protocol field → Set source/dest IP → Set identification → Set TTL → [if > MTU] Fragment → Calculate checksum → Route lookup → Get next-hop MAC (ARP) → Hand to Ethernet layer. Shows fragmentation decision point.

diagrams.title: TTL Handling and Time Exceeded
diagrams.description: Shows packet forwarding with TTL decrement: Packet arrives with TTL=64 → Decrement to 63 → Forward. Contrast with TTL=1 packet → Decrement to 0 → DROP → Send ICMP Time Exceeded back to source. Explains traceroute mechanism.

diagrams.title: TCP Segment Byte Layout
diagrams.description: Complete TCP header with byte offsets: Source Port (16b), Dest Port (16b), Sequence Number (32b), Acknowledgment Number (32b), Data Offset (4b), Reserved (3b), Flags (9b: NS, CWR, ECE, URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN), Window (16b), Checksum (16b), Urgent Pointer (16b), Options (variable). Shows minimum 20-byte header.

diagrams.title: TCP Pseudo-Header for Checksum
diagrams.description: The pseudo-header that TCP checksum covers: Source IP (32b), Destination IP (32b), Zero (8b), Protocol=6 (8b), TCP Length (16b), then TCP header + data. Shows why TCP layer needs IP addresses—breaking layer separation.

diagrams.title: TCP State Machine (RFC 793 Figure 6)
diagrams.description: Complete 11-state diagram: CLOSED → LISTEN (passive open) → SYN_RECEIVED → ESTABLISHED → FIN_WAIT_1 → FIN_WAIT_2 → TIME_WAIT → CLOSED. Also: CLOSED → SYN_SENT (active open) → ESTABLISHED → CLOSE_WAIT → LAST_ACK → CLOSED. Shows ALL transitions with triggering events (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK, FIN, RST, timeout).

diagrams.title: TCP 3-Way Handshake Sequence
diagrams.description: Timeline: Client SYN (seq=ISN_C) → Server SYN-ACK (seq=ISN_S, ack=ISN_C+1) → Client ACK (seq=ISN_C+1, ack=ISN_S+1) → ESTABLISHED. Shows state transitions at each step: CLOSED→SYN_SENT→ESTABLISHED (client), LISTEN→SYN_RECEIVED→ESTABLISHED (server).

diagrams.title: TCP 4-Way Connection Teardown
diagrams.description: Timeline: Active closer FIN → Passive closer ACK → Passive closer FIN → Active closer ACK → TIME_WAIT (2*MSL) → CLOSED. Shows simultaneous close scenario. Explains why TIME_WAIT exists (absorb delayed segments).

diagrams.title: TCP MSS Option Negotiation
diagrams.description: SYN segment with MSS option: Kind=2, Length=4, MSS Value (e.g., 1460). Shows how MSS is extracted from SYN and SYN-ACK, and how sender limits segment payload to min(local MSS, remote MSS). Default 536 bytes when absent.

diagrams.title: TCP Options Parsing Logic
diagrams.description: Options are (Kind, Length, Value) tuples. Shows parsing loop: read Kind → if Kind < 2 handle specially → else read Length → read (Length-2) bytes of Value → advance. Shows common options: EOL (0), NOP (1), MSS (2,4), Window Scale (3,3), Timestamp (8,10).

diagrams.title: TCP Connection Table Structure
diagrams.description: Hash table keyed by 4-tuple (local IP, local port, remote IP, remote port). Each entry contains: TCP state, sequence numbers (SND.UNA, SND.NXT, SND.WND, RCV.NXT, RCV.WND), timers, send/recv buffers. Shows connection lookup on segment arrival.

diagrams.title: Modulo 2^32 Sequence Number Comparison
diagrams.description: Visual explanation of SEQ_LT, SEQ_LE, SEQ_GT, SEQ_GE macros using circular number line 0 to 2^32-1. Shows why (uint32_t)(a - b) < (uint32_t)(c - d) works for comparison. Example: seq=0xFFFFFF00, ack=0x00000100 → SEQ_LT(seq, ack) is TRUE despite numeric wrap.

diagrams.title: TCP Simultaneous Open Scenario
diagrams.description: Rare but required scenario: Both sides send SYN simultaneously. State transitions: CLOSED → SYN_SENT → SYN_RECEIVED (both) → ESTABLISHED. Shows packet exchange: SYN from A, SYN from B, SYN-ACK from both.

diagrams.title: RST Segment Handling by State
diagrams.description: Decision tree for RST: (1) In CLOSED/LISTEN → ignore, (2) In SYN_RECEIVED → return to LISTEN, (3) In ESTABLISHED/FIN_WAIT* → abort connection, notify user, (4) In CLOSE_WAIT → abort. Shows RST generation when segment doesn't match any connection.

diagrams.title: Initial Sequence Number Generation
diagrams.description: RFC 6528 ISN generation: ISN = M + F(local_ip, local_port, remote_ip, remote_port, secret). Shows why predictable ISNs are security risk (spoofing) and why time-based or cryptographic generation is required.

diagrams.title: TCP Segment Receive Processing
diagrams.description: Flowchart: Verify checksum → Lookup connection → Validate against state → [if SYN_SENT] Complete handshake → [if ESTABLISHED] Check sequence → [if acceptable] Process data/ACK/FIN → Update window → Send response. Shows drop points.

diagrams.title: Sliding Window Protocol Concept
diagrams.description: Byte stream with window overlay showing: Sent and ACKed | Sent not ACKed (in flight) | Usable window (can send) | Cannot send yet. Shows window sliding right as ACKs arrive. Variables: SND.UNA, SND.NXT, SND.WND.

diagrams.title: Effective Window Calculation
diagrams.description: Formula: EffectiveWindow = min(cwnd, rwnd) - (SND.NXT - SND.UNA). Shows both congestion window (network limit) and receiver window (buffer limit) constraining send rate. Example with cwnd=64KB, rwnd=32KB, in-flight=8KB → can send 24KB.

diagrams.title: Sender Buffer as Ring Structure
diagrams.description: Circular buffer for send-side: [ACKed data][Unacked data in flight][Usable space][Empty]. Pointers: SND.UNA (oldest unacked), SND.NXT (next to send), buffer_end. Shows retransmission from SND.UNA on timeout.

diagrams.title: Receiver Out-of-Order Buffer
diagrams.description: Receive buffer with gaps: [Received 1-100][Gap 101-200][Received 201-300][Gap 301-400][Received 401-500]. Shows RCV.NXT pointing to first gap, and how gaps fill in. ACK sent with RCV.NXT (cumulative ACK for 100).

diagrams.title: RTT Measurement and RTO Calculation
diagrams.description: Timeline showing RTT sample: Send segment at T1, receive ACK at T2, RTT = T2 - T1. Jacobson's algorithm: SRTT = (7/8)*SRTT + (1/8)*RTT, RTTVAR = (3/4)*RTTVAR + (1/4)*|SRTT-RTT|, RTO = SRTT + 4*RTTVAR. Karn's algorithm: skip samples from retransmitted segments.

diagrams.title: Retransmission Timer State Machine
diagrams.description: Timer states: STOPPED → RUNNING (when data sent) → EXPIRED (RTO elapsed) → [retransmit, RTO *= 2] → RUNNING. Timer stops when SND.UNA == SND.NXT (all data ACKed). Shows exponential backoff on repeated timeouts.

diagrams.title: Timer Wheel for Efficient Retransmission
diagrams.description: Hashed timing wheel: array of N buckets, each bucket is a list of timer entries. Current time pointer advances each tick. O(1) insertion, O(1) tick processing. Shows why per-segment timers are O(n) and timer wheel is required.

diagrams.title: Congestion Window Growth Over Time
diagrams.description: Graph of cwnd vs time: Exponential growth in slow start (1→2→4→8→16 MSS), then linear growth in congestion avoidance (+1 MSS/RTT). Shows loss event causing cwnd drop to 1 and ssthresh set to cwnd/2.

diagrams.title: Slow Start vs Congestion Avoidance
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison: Slow start (cwnd < ssthresh): cwnd += MSS per ACK (exponential). Congestion avoidance (cwnd >= ssthresh): cwnd += MSS*MSS/cwnd per ACK (linear ~1 MSS/RTT). Shows why 'slow start' is actually fast.

diagrams.title: Fast Retransmit on 3 Duplicate ACKs
diagrams.description: Timeline: Send seq 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Segment 2 lost. Receiver sends ACK 2 (dup), ACK 2 (dup), ACK 2 (dup). After 3rd duplicate ACK, sender immediately retransmits segment 2 without waiting for RTO. Shows dup ACK count mechanism.

diagrams.title: Fast Recovery State Transitions
diagrams.description: Flow: 3 dup ACKs → ssthresh = cwnd/2 → retransmit lost segment → cwnd = ssthresh + 3*MSS → for each additional dup ACK: cwnd += MSS → when new ACK arrives: cwnd = ssthresh, exit fast recovery. Shows window inflation/deflation.

diagrams.title: Zero Window Probing
diagrams.description: Scenario: Receiver buffer full, sends rwnd=0. Sender stops, sends 1-byte probe periodically. When receiver frees space, sends window update. Shows probe timing (persist timer) and how connection resumes.

diagrams.title: Silly Window Syndrome Prevention
diagrams.description: Problem: Receiver advertises tiny windows (1 byte), sender sends tiny segments (inefficient). Solution (Clark): Don't advertise window < MSS. Sender-side Nagle: don't send tiny segments if unacked data exists. Shows pathological case vs fixed behavior.

diagrams.title: End-to-End 1MB File Transfer Test
diagrams.description: Complete test scenario: Client opens connection → sends 1MB file → server receives and writes to buffer → connection closes → SHA-256 comparison of sent vs received. Shows all mechanisms working together: handshake, window flow, retransmission, congestion control, orderly close.

diagrams.title: TCP Sender State Variables
diagrams.description: Complete variable set for sender: SND.UNA (oldest unacked), SND.NXT (next to send), SND.WND (receiver's window), SND.UP (urgent pointer), SND.WL1/WL2 (window update seq/ack), ISS (initial send seq). Also cwnd, ssthresh, RTO, SRTT, RTTVAR. Shows how they interact.

diagrams.title: TCP Receiver State Variables
diagrams.description: Complete variable set for receiver: RCV.NXT (next expected), RCV.WND (advertised window), RCV.UP (urgent pointer), IRS (initial recv seq). Shows how RCV.NXT advances as in-order data arrives and how RCV.WND is calculated from buffer space.

diagrams.title: Nagle's Algorithm Decision Tree
diagrams.description: Decision tree: Data to send? → Is there unacked data? → YES: Buffer data until ACK arrives or buffer >= MSS. NO: Send immediately. Shows interaction with delayed ACKs causing 200ms delays (the Nagle + delayed ACK problem).

diagrams.title: Complete Packet Flow for One Segment
diagrams.description: Trace one segment through entire stack: Application write() → TCP segment builder → TCP checksum (pseudo-header) → IP header builder → IP checksum → Route lookup → ARP cache lookup → Ethernet header builder → TAP device write(). Receive path in reverse.

diagrams.title: Wireshark Packet Dissection Reference
diagrams.description: Shows how Wireshark displays each layer for a TCP packet: Frame (interface, timestamp), Ethernet (MACs, type), IP (all fields), TCP (all fields including options), Data. Maps Wireshark output to our parser output for debugging.

anchor_registry.anchor-stack-global: Global stack view linking all milestones
anchor_registry.anchor-ethernet-arp: Milestone 1: Ethernet frame handling and ARP resolution
anchor_registry.anchor-ip-icmp: Milestone 2: IP layer processing and ICMP messaging
anchor_registry.anchor-tcp-connection: Milestone 3: TCP connection state machine and handshake
anchor_registry.anchor-tcp-reliable: Milestone 4: Sliding window, retransmission, congestion control

packet_processing.cache_behavior: Each packet touches L1/L2 cache for header parsing; sequential memory access for payload is prefetch-friendly; random access for routing table lookups can cause cache misses
packet_processing.branch_prediction: Protocol parsing has predictable branches (header format is fixed); state machine transitions are data-dependent and may mispredict on edge cases
packet_processing.simd_opportunity: Checksum calculation can use SIMD (parallel 16-bit adds); payload copying benefits from vectorized memcpy
packet_processing.memory_pattern: Packet buffers should be cache-line aligned (64B); sequential reads for parsing; scatter-gather for header assembly

timer_management.cache_behavior: Timer wheel is cache-friendly (array access); per-connection timers in hash table may cause random access
timer_management.branch_prediction: Timer expiration checks are highly predictable (most timers don't fire)
timer_management.tlb_pressure: Many connections = many buffer pages = potential TLB misses; consider huge pages for production

congestion_control.branch_prediction: State transitions (slow start → avoidance) are rare per-connection; dup ACK counting is predictable
congestion_control.floating_point: Jacobson's algorithm uses integer math (scaled by 8 or 256) to avoid FP in kernel context

cross_domain_connections.connection: Both use broadcast (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) for initial configuration; DHCP adds transaction IDs and relay agents
PMTUD sends packets with DF (Don't Fragment) flag and uses ICMP Fragmentation Needed responses to find optimal MTU
TLS adds crypto on top; 1.3 combines TCP handshake with crypto in 1-RTT (0-RTT with session resumption)
BBR models the network pipe (bandwidth × RTT) instead of reacting to loss; uses delivery rate estimation
QUIC reimplements TCP's state machine in userspace over UDP, adding connection migration and stream multiplexing
Both use modulo arithmetic for wraparound; both need careful comparison operators for 'before' and 'after'
Adaptive bitrate streaming uses similar window concepts—buffer what you can, pause when full, resume when space available

tdd_blueprint.design_vision: A complete userspace TCP/IP network stack implementing Ethernet framing, ARP resolution, IPv4 routing with fragmentation, ICMP diagnostics, and full TCP with 3-way handshake, sliding window flow control, adaptive retransmission, and congestion control. The stack bridges the fundamental tension between software's desire for reliable byte streams and hardware's reality of fixed-size frames, unreliable delivery, and limited buffer space.

modules.name: Ethernet & ARP
modules.description: Implements Layer 2 packet I/O via TAP device, Ethernet frame parsing with VLAN handling, and ARP request/reply for IP-to-MAC address resolution with caching and pending packet queuing.

specs.inputs: Raw Ethernet frames from TAP device file descriptor
specs.outputs: Parsed Ethernet headers, resolved MAC addresses, ARP cache lookups, outbound frames with correct MAC addresses
specs.abstractions: TAP device as virtual network interface, ARP cache as IP→MAC mapping with TTL expiration, pending queue for packets awaiting resolution
error_categories: TAP device open failure (permissions, device not found)
Frame too short (<14 bytes minimum header)
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded event loop with poll() for I/O multiplexing. ARP cache access is synchronous. Pending queues are processed in-order on resolution.
performance_targets: ARP cache lookup: O(1) hash table, <100ns
Support 256+ ARP cache entries in <5KB memory

diagrams.title: TCP/IP Stack Architecture: Satellite View
diagrams.description: Layered view showing Ethernet→IP→TCP→Application data flow, highlighting where each module fits in the stack

diagrams.title: Ethernet Send/Receive Data Path
diagrams.description: Complete path from application through TAP device to wire, showing read/write flow and kernel bypass

diagrams.title: TAP Device Architecture
diagrams.description: Virtual network interface connecting userspace to kernel networking, showing file descriptor, ioctl configuration, and frame queuing

diagrams.title: Ethernet Frame Byte Layout
diagrams.description: Memory layout with byte offsets: Preamble(7), SFD(1), DstMAC(6), SrcMAC(6), VLAN(4 optional), EtherType(2), Payload(46-1500), FCS(4)

diagrams.title: Ethernet Frame Parsing State Machine
diagrams.description: Decision tree for parsing frames: check length, extract MACs, check EtherType for VLAN, calculate payload offset

diagrams.title: ARP Request/Reply Message Flow
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram showing broadcast request, all hosts receive, target responds with unicast reply

diagrams.title: ARP Cache Data Structure
diagrams.description: Hash table with IP keys, MAC values, expiration timestamps. Shows collision handling and LRU eviction strategy

diagrams.title: ARP Packet Structure Memory Layout
diagrams.description: 28-byte ARP packet: hw_type(2), proto_type(2), hw_addr_len(1), proto_addr_len(1), opcode(2), sender_hw(6), sender_proto(4), target_hw(6), target_proto(4)

diagrams.title: Pending Queue Flow
diagrams.description: Data flow: outbound packet arrives, ARP miss triggers queue, resolution completes, queued packets flushed

diagrams.title: Main Event Loop State Machine
diagrams.description: Poll wait → read frame → parse Ethernet → dispatch by EtherType (ARP/IPv4) → handle → loop

modules.name: IP Layer & ICMP
modules.description: Implements IPv4 packet parsing with checksum verification, IP fragmentation reassembly with timeout, static routing table with longest-prefix match, and ICMP echo request/reply (ping) with error message generation.

specs.inputs: IPv4 packets from Ethernet payload, routing table configuration
specs.outputs: Parsed IP headers, reassembled datagrams, ICMP echo replies, ICMP time exceeded/destination unreachable errors, routing decisions
specs.abstractions: IP header as 20-60 byte structure with checksum, fragmentation as offset-based reassembly buffer, routing table as prefix→gateway mapping, ICMP as error signaling layer
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded. Fragment reassembly contexts are independent. Routing table is read-mostly with rare updates.
performance_targets: Routing lookup: O(n) linear for small tables, <1μs for 256 routes
Fragment reassembly buffer: <64KB per context, max 64 contexts

implementation_phases.name: Routing Table with Longest Prefix Match

diagrams.title: IP Layer Receive Processing Path
diagrams.description: Flow from Ethernet payload through parsing, checksum, fragmentation check, routing decision, delivery to upper layer

diagrams.title: IPv4 Header Byte Layout
diagrams.description: 20-byte minimum header: Version(4b), IHL(4b), TOS(1), TotalLen(2), ID(2), Flags(3b)/FragOffset(13b), TTL(1), Proto(1), Checksum(2), SrcIP(4), DstIP(4)

diagrams.title: IP Checksum Calculation (RFC 1071)
diagrams.description: Algorithm steps: sum 16-bit words, fold carries, ones complement. Verification shows 0xFFFF for valid checksum

diagrams.title: IP Fragmentation and Reassembly
diagrams.description: Shows original datagram split into fragments with offsets, MF flags. Reassembly combines by offset into buffer

diagrams.title: Fragment Reassembly Buffer Structure
diagrams.description: Per-datagram context: src/dst IP, ID, protocol, linked list of fragment nodes with offset/length/data, expiration timer

diagrams.title: Routing Table with Longest Prefix Match
diagrams.description: Trie structure showing how /30 beats /24 beats /8 for same destination. Shows bit-by-bit descent

diagrams.title: ICMP Echo Request/Reply (Ping) Flow
diagrams.description: Sequence: Echo Request arrives with ID/Seq, validate checksum, build Echo Reply with same ID/Seq, send back

diagrams.title: TTL Handling and Time Exceeded
diagrams.description: Per-hop TTL decrement, zero detection triggers ICMP Time Exceeded with original header+8 bytes

diagrams.title: ICMP Packet Memory Layout
diagrams.description: 8-byte base header: Type(1), Code(1), Checksum(2), Rest(4 varies by type). Echo adds ID(2)+Seq(2). Error adds unused(4)+original header

diagrams.title: IP Forwarding Decision Tree
diagrams.description: Check destination is-us → no → check routing → decrement TTL → zero? → ICMP Time Exceeded : forward

modules.name: TCP Connection Management
modules.description: Implements TCP segment parsing with pseudo-header checksum, the complete 11-state machine per RFC 793, 3-way handshake for connection establishment, MSS option negotiation, 4-way FIN handshake for graceful close, TIME_WAIT state with 2*MSL timer, RST handling, and connection table for concurrent sessions.

specs.inputs: TCP segments from IP layer payload, application connect/listen/close requests
specs.outputs: TCP segments with correct sequence numbers, ACKs, SYNs, FINs, RSTs; connection state transitions; established connections ready for data transfer
specs.abstractions: TCP segment as header+options+data with pseudo-header checksum, connection as 4-tuple with sequence number state, state machine as event-driven transitions, connection table as 4-tuple→state mapping
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded event loop. Multiple connections managed via connection table. Each connection has independent state machine. Timer events processed centrally.
performance_targets: Connection lookup: O(n) linear, <1μs for 1024 connections

diagrams.title: TCP State Machine (RFC 793 Figure 6)
diagrams.description: Complete 11-state diagram: CLOSED→LISTEN→SYN_RECEIVED→ESTABLISHED and CLOSED→SYN_SENT→ESTABLISHED with all close paths

diagrams.title: TCP Segment Byte Layout
diagrams.description: 20-byte minimum header: SrcPort(2), DstPort(2), SeqNum(4), AckNum(4), DataOffset(4b)/Reserved(4b)/Flags(8), Window(2), Checksum(2), UrgentPtr(2)

diagrams.title: TCP Pseudo-Header for Checksum
diagrams.description: 12-byte pseudo-header: SrcIP(4), DstIP(4), Zero(1), Protocol(1), TCPLength(2). Prepended to TCP segment for checksum

diagrams.title: TCP 3-Way Handshake Sequence
diagrams.description: Client SYN(seq=ISN) → Server SYN-ACK(seq=ISN, ack=ISN+1) → Client ACK(ack=ISN+1). Shows state transitions at each step

diagrams.title: TCP 4-Way Connection Teardown
diagrams.description: Active FIN → ACK → Passive FIN → ACK. Shows FIN_WAIT_1→FIN_WAIT_2→TIME_WAIT and CLOSE_WAIT→LAST_ACK paths

diagrams.title: Modulo 2^32 Sequence Number Comparison
diagrams.description: Visual explanation of SEQ_LT/SEQ_GT macros using clock face analogy. Shows wraparound at 2^32-1→0

diagrams.title: TCP Options Parsing Logic
diagrams.description: State machine for parsing option bytes: Kind=0 (EOL), Kind=1 (NOP), Kind=2 (MSS with Length=4), etc.

diagrams.title: TCP MSS Option Negotiation
diagrams.description: SYN carries MSS option, peer responds with SYN-ACK carrying its MSS. Sender uses min(local, peer) MSS

diagrams.title: TCP Connection Table Structure
diagrams.description: Array of connection entries indexed by 4-tuple hash. Each entry: local/remote IP:port, state, sequence variables, buffers

diagrams.title: RST Segment Handling by State
diagrams.description: Decision tree: RST in SYN_RECEIVED→LISTEN, RST in ESTABLISHED→CLOSED (abort), RST out-of-window→ignore

diagrams.title: Simultaneous Open Scenario
diagrams.description: Both sides send SYN simultaneously: CLOSED→SYN_SENT→SYN_RECEIVED→ESTABLISHED for both

diagrams.title: Initial Sequence Number Generation
diagrams.description: ISN = hash(clock + connection_tuple). Shows clock component for uniqueness, tuple hash for per-connection variation

diagrams.title: TCP Segment Receive Processing
diagrams.description: Flow: verify checksum → lookup connection → check state → validate sequence → process flags → update state → send response

modules.name: TCP Reliable Delivery & Flow Control
modules.description: Implements sliding window protocol for sender and receiver, out-of-order segment buffering, adaptive RTO calculation using Jacobson's algorithm with Karn's amendment, retransmission with exponential backoff, complete congestion control (slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit, fast recovery), zero-window probing, Nagle's algorithm, and end-to-end file transfer verification.

specs.inputs: Application data to send, incoming TCP segments with ACKs, timer events
specs.outputs: Reliable, in-order byte stream to application, ACKs for received data, retransmissions for lost segments, congestion-controlled sending rate
specs.abstractions: Send buffer as ring with SND.UNA/SND.NXT pointers, receive buffer with out-of-order queue, RTO as smoothed RTT estimate, congestion window as network-imposed limit, sliding window as intersection of cwnd, rwnd, and in-flight data
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded event loop. Timer heap manages retransmit, persist, and delayed ACK timers. Send/receive buffers are per-connection. Congestion state updated atomically per ACK.
performance_targets: Send buffer ring operations: <50ns per byte
Timer heap operations: O(log n) insert/delete
Out-of-order check: O(n) for n buffered segments
1MB transfer: complete in <5s on localhost, <30s with 0.1% loss

diagrams.title: Sliding Window Protocol Concept
diagrams.description: Visual window showing: acknowledged (left of window), in-flight (inside window), not-yet-sent (right edge), beyond window (cannot send)

diagrams.title: Effective Window Calculation
diagrams.description: Formula: usable = min(cwnd, rwnd) - (SND.NXT - SND.UNA). Shows intersection of congestion, receiver, and in-flight constraints

diagrams.title: Sender Buffer as Ring Structure
diagrams.description: Circular buffer with head=SND.UNA offset, tail=app write position, snd_nxt=next send position. Shows wraparound handling

diagrams.title: Receiver Out-of-Order Buffer
diagrams.description: In-order region followed by gap, then OOO segments stored. Shows RCV.NXT advancing when gap fills

diagrams.title: RTT Measurement and RTO Calculation
diagrams.description: Timeline showing segment send, ACK receive, RTT sample. Jacobson formula: RTO = SRTT + 4*RTTVAR with alpha=1/8, beta=1/4

diagrams.title: Retransmission Timer State Machine
diagrams.description: States: IDLE → ARMED (data sent) → EXPIRED (timeout) → BACKOFF (double RTO) → RESET (ACK received)

diagrams.title: Congestion Window Growth Over Time
diagrams.description: Graph showing slow start exponential growth until ssthresh, then linear congestion avoidance. Drops on loss events

diagrams.title: Slow Start vs Congestion Avoidance
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison: Slow Start adds 1 MSS per ACK (exponential), Congestion Avoidance adds MSS²/cwnd per ACK (linear ~1 MSS/RTT)

diagrams.title: Fast Retransmit on 3 Duplicate ACKs
diagrams.description: Sequence: Segments 1,2,3,4,5 sent. 3 lost. Receiver ACKs 1, then dup ACKs for 1 when 4,5 arrive. Sender sees 3 dup ACKs, retransmits 3

diagrams.title: Fast Recovery Flow
diagrams.description: State machine: 3 dup ACKs → ssthresh=cwnd/2, cwnd=ssthresh+3 → inflate cwnd per dup ACK → new ACK exits recovery, cwnd=ssthresh

diagrams.title: Zero Window Probing
diagrams.description: Receiver advertises 0 → sender starts persist timer → sends 1-byte probe → receiver ACKs with window → resume or backoff

diagrams.title: Nagle's Algorithm Decision Tree
diagrams.description: Can send? Full segment OR no unacked data OR PSH flag OR nagle disabled → YES, else wait for ACK

diagrams.title: Silly Window Syndrome Prevention
diagrams.description: Receiver: don't advertise small windows (Clark). Sender: don't send small segments with unacked data (Nagle)

diagrams.title: Complete Packet Flow for One Segment
diagrams.description: End-to-end: App send → buffer → segment → IP → Ethernet → wire → receive → parse → validate → buffer → app receive. Shows latency at each stage

diagrams.title: End-to-End 1MB File Transfer Test
diagrams.description: Test flow: generate 1MB data → SHA-256 hash → send via TCP → receive → SHA-256 verify → compare hashes

diagrams.title: ACK Processing State Machine
diagrams.description: ACK arrives → new data ACKed? → update buffer, measure RTT, grow cwnd, check fast recovery exit : duplicate? → increment dup count, check fast retransmit

diagrams.title: Timer Heap Structure
diagrams.description: Min-heap of timer entries sorted by expire_time. Shows heapify operations for insert/remove. Timer types: retransmit, persist, time_wait, delayed_ack

tdd_accumulated_md:
# TDD

A complete userspace TCP/IP network stack implementing Ethernet framing, ARP resolution, IPv4 routing with fragmentation, ICMP diagnostics, and full TCP with 3-way handshake, sliding window flow control, adaptive retransmission, and congestion control. The stack bridges the fundamental tension between software's desire for reliable byte streams and hardware's reality of fixed-size frames, unreliable delivery, and limited buffer space.



<!-- TDD_MOD_ID: build-tcp-stack-m1 -->
# Technical Design Document: Ethernet & ARP Module
**Module ID:** `build-tcp-stack-m1`  
**Primary Language:** C  
**Domain:** Systems / Low-Level Networking
---
## 1. Module Charter
This module implements Layer 2 networking infrastructure: TAP device management for raw Ethernet frame I/O, Ethernet frame parsing with optional 802.1Q VLAN tag handling, and the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) for IP-to-MAC address resolution. The module bridges the gap between the kernel's network stack and userspace by providing direct access to Ethernet frames through a virtual network interface.
**What this module DOES:**
- Opens and configures a TAP device for raw frame transmission/reception
- Parses Ethernet frames extracting destination MAC, source MAC, EtherType, and payload
- Handles IEEE 802.1Q VLAN-tagged frames transparently
- Implements ARP request/reply per RFC 826 for IP-to-MAC resolution
- Maintains an ARP cache with TTL-based expiration
- Queues outbound packets pending ARP resolution
**What this module does NOT do:**
- IP packet processing (handled by Layer 3 module)
- TCP/UDP processing (handled by Layer 4 module)
- Physical hardware access (TAP is virtual)
- Routing decisions (Layer 3 responsibility)
**Dependencies:**
- Upstream: Linux kernel TAP device support (`/dev/net/tun`)
- Downstream: IP layer module receives parsed frames via EtherType dispatch
**Invariants:**
- All multi-byte fields in network byte order (big-endian) on wire, converted to host order in memory
- ARP cache entries expire after 60 seconds by default
- Frames shorter than 14 bytes are silently dropped
- Broadcast MAC (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) is always accepted
---
## 2. File Structure
```
tcp_stack/
├── include/
│   ├── eth.h              # [1] Ethernet frame structures and parser
│   ├── arp.h              # [2] ARP structures and cache
│   └── tap.h              # [3] TAP device interface
├── src/
│   ├── eth.c              # [4] Ethernet frame implementation
│   ├── arp.c              # [5] ARP implementation
│   ├── tap.c              # [6] TAP device implementation
│   └── main.c             # [7] Main event loop
└── tests/
    ├── test_eth.c         # [8] Ethernet parser tests
    ├── test_arp.c         # [9] ARP cache tests
    └── test_integration.c # [10] End-to-end tests
```
**Creation Order:** Files 1-3 (headers) first, then 4-7 (implementation), finally 8-10 (tests).
---
## 3. Complete Data Model
### 3.1 Ethernet Header Structure
```c
// include/eth.h
#include <stdint.h>
#include <stddef.h>
#define ETH_ADDR_LEN      6     // MAC address length in bytes
#define ETH_HDR_LEN       14    // Dest MAC (6) + Src MAC (6) + EtherType (2)
#define ETH_VLAN_HDR_LEN  4     // VLAN tag: TCI (2) + EtherType (2)
#define ETH_MTU           1500  // Maximum Transmission Unit
#define ETH_FRAME_MAX     (ETH_HDR_LEN + ETH_VLAN_HDR_LEN + ETH_MTU + 4) // +4 for potential padding
// EtherType values (network byte order after conversion)
#define ETH_TYPE_IPV4     0x0800
#define ETH_TYPE_ARP      0x0806
#define ETH_TYPE_VLAN     0x8100  // 802.1Q VLAN tag
#define ETH_TYPE_IPV6     0x86DD
/**
 * Ethernet frame header (14 bytes minimum).
 * 
 * MEMORY LAYOUT (byte offsets):
 * ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 * │ Offset │ Size │ Field          │ Description               │
 * ├────────┼──────┼────────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
 * │ 0x00   │ 6    │ dst_mac        │ Destination MAC address   │
 * │ 0x06   │ 6    │ src_mac        │ Source MAC address        │
 * │ 0x0C   │ 2    │ ethertype      │ Protocol identifier       │
 * └────────┴──────┴────────────────┴───────────────────────────┘
 * 
 * CRITICAL: ethertype is in NETWORK BYTE ORDER (big-endian).
 * Use ntohs() to read, htons() to write.
 */
struct eth_hdr {
    uint8_t  dst_mac[ETH_ADDR_LEN];   // Offset 0x00: Destination MAC
    uint8_t  src_mac[ETH_ADDR_LEN];   // Offset 0x06: Source MAC
    uint16_t ethertype;               // Offset 0x0C: EtherType (big-endian)
} __attribute__((packed));
static_assert(sizeof(struct eth_hdr) == ETH_HDR_LEN, "eth_hdr size mismatch");
/**
 * IEEE 802.1Q VLAN tag (4 bytes).
 * Appears between source MAC and EtherType when ethertype == 0x8100.
 * 
 * MEMORY LAYOUT:
 * ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 * │ Offset │ Size │ Field          │ Description               │
 * ├────────┼──────┼────────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
 * │ 0x00   │ 2    │ tci            │ Tag Control Information   │
 * │ 0x02   │ 2    │ ethertype      │ Inner EtherType           │
 * └────────┴──────┴────────────────┴───────────────────────────┘
 * 
 * TCI bit layout:
 * ┌─────┬─────┬───────────────┐
 * │ PCP │ DEI │   VLAN ID     │
 * │3b   │1b   │   12b         │
 * └─────┴─────┴───────────────┘
 */
struct vlan_tag {
    uint16_t tci;        // Tag Control Info: PCP(3) + DEI(1) + VID(12)
    uint16_t ethertype;  // Inner EtherType (big-endian)
} __attribute__((packed));
static_assert(sizeof(struct vlan_tag) == ETH_VLAN_HDR_LEN, "vlan_tag size mismatch");
```
### 3.2 ARP Structures
```c
// include/arp.h
#include <stdint.h>
#include <time.h>
// ARP hardware types
#define ARP_HW_ETHERNET     1
#define ARP_HW_EXP_ETHERNET 6
// ARP protocol types (same as Ethernet EtherType)
#define ARP_PROTO_IP        0x0800
// ARP operation codes
#define ARP_OP_REQUEST      1
#define ARP_OP_REPLY        2
// Cache configuration
#define ARP_CACHE_SIZE      256
#define ARP_CACHE_TTL       60    // Seconds before entry expires
/**
 * ARP packet structure (28 bytes for Ethernet+IPv4).
 * RFC 826 format.
 * 
 * MEMORY LAYOUT:
 * ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
 * │ Offset │ Size │ Field            │ Description                     │
 * ├────────┼──────┼──────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
 * │ 0x00   │ 2    │ hw_type          │ Hardware type (1=Ethernet)      │
 * │ 0x02   │ 2    │ proto_type       │ Protocol type (0x0800=IPv4)     │
 * │ 0x04   │ 1    │ hw_addr_len      │ HW addr length (6 for Ethernet) │
 * │ 0x05   │ 1    │ proto_addr_len   │ Proto addr length (4 for IPv4)  │
 * │ 0x06   │ 2    │ opcode           │ Operation (1=req, 2=reply)      │
 * │ 0x08   │ 6    │ sender_hw        │ Sender's MAC address            │
 * │ 0x0E   │ 4    │ sender_proto     │ Sender's IP address             │
 * │ 0x12   │ 6    │ target_hw        │ Target's MAC (0 for requests)   │
 * │ 0x18   │ 4    │ target_proto     │ Target's IP address             │
 * └────────┴──────┴──────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘
 * 
 * ALL multi-byte fields are NETWORK BYTE ORDER.
 */
struct arp_hdr {
    uint16_t hw_type;                   // Offset 0x00
    uint16_t proto_type;                // Offset 0x02
    uint8_t  hw_addr_len;               // Offset 0x04
    uint8_t  proto_addr_len;            // Offset 0x05
    uint16_t opcode;                    // Offset 0x06
    uint8_t  sender_hw[ETH_ADDR_LEN];   // Offset 0x08
    uint8_t  sender_proto[4];           // Offset 0x0E
    uint8_t  target_hw[ETH_ADDR_LEN];   // Offset 0x12
    uint8_t  target_proto[4];           // Offset 0x18
} __attribute__((packed));
#define ARP_PKT_LEN sizeof(struct arp_hdr)
static_assert(ARP_PKT_LEN == 28, "arp_hdr size mismatch");
/**
 * ARP cache entry.
 * 
 * CACHE LINE ANALYSIS (64-byte cache line):
 * - ip (4) + mac (6) + padding (2) + expires_at (8) + valid (4) = 24 bytes
 * - Fits 2 entries per cache line with 16 bytes to spare
 * - For L1 cache optimization: 256 entries × 24 bytes = 6KB
 * - Fits comfortably in L1 (typically 32KB)
 */
struct arp_entry {
    uint32_t ip;                    // Key: IP address (host byte order)
    uint8_t  mac[ETH_ADDR_LEN];     // Value: MAC address
    uint8_t  _pad[2];               // Padding for alignment
    time_t   expires_at;            // Expiration timestamp
    int      valid;                 // Entry validity flag
};
/**
 * ARP cache hash table.
 * Simple open addressing with linear probe.
 */
struct arp_cache {
    struct arp_entry entries[ARP_CACHE_SIZE];
};
/**
 * Pending packet queue entry.
 */
struct pending_packet {
    uint8_t  data[ETH_FRAME_MAX];
    size_t   len;
    int      valid;
};
/**
 * Queue for packets awaiting ARP resolution.
 */
struct pending_queue {
    uint32_t ip;                              // IP being resolved
    struct pending_packet packets[16];        // Max 16 packets per IP
    int      head;                            // Write position
    int      count;                           // Packets queued
    time_t   request_sent;                    // When ARP request was sent
    int      retry_count;                     // Retries attempted
    int      in_use;
};
#define PENDING_QUEUES_MAX 32
struct pending_queues {
    struct pending_queue queues[PENDING_QUEUES_MAX];
    int count;
};
```

## 4. Interface Contracts
### 4.1 TAP Device Functions
```c
/**
 * Open and configure a TAP device.
 * 
 * @param dev       Pointer to tap_device struct to initialize
 * @param ip_addr   IP address string (e.g., "10.0.0.1")
 * @param netmask   Netmask string (e.g., "255.255.255.0")
 * @return          0 on success, -1 on error
 * 
 * @error EPERM     Caller lacks CAP_NET_ADMIN (run as root)
 * @error ENODEV    /dev/net/tun does not exist
 * @error ENOMEM    Insufficient memory for interface
 * @error EEXIST    Interface name already in use
 * 
 * POSTCONDITIONS:
 * - dev->fd is a valid file descriptor for read/write
 * - dev->name contains the actual interface name
 * - Interface is UP and RUNNING
 * - IP address and netmask are configured
 */
int tap_open(struct tap_device *dev, const char *ip_addr, const char *netmask);
/**
 * Close a TAP device and release resources.
 * 
 * @param dev       Pointer to tap_device struct
 * 
 * POSTCONDITIONS:
 * - File descriptor is closed
 * - Interface may persist in kernel (use 'ip link delete' to remove)
 */
void tap_close(struct tap_device *dev);
/**
 * Read a raw Ethernet frame from the TAP device.
 * 
 * @param dev       TAP device context
 * @param buffer    Output buffer (must be at least ETH_FRAME_MAX bytes)
 * @param buf_len   Size of buffer
 * @return          Bytes read on success, -1 on error, 0 on EOF
 * 
 * @error EAGAIN    Non-blocking mode and no data available
 * @error EMSGSIZE  Frame larger than buffer (truncated)
 * 
 * NOTE: Returns raw Ethernet frame starting with dst_mac.
 * Does NOT include preamble, SFD, or FCS (handled by kernel/NIC).
 */
ssize_t tap_read(struct tap_device *dev, uint8_t *buffer, size_t buf_len);
/**
 * Write a raw Ethernet frame to the TAP device.
 * 
 * @param dev       TAP device context
 * @param buffer    Frame data (complete Ethernet frame)
 * @param len       Frame length (14-1518 bytes typically)
 * @return          Bytes written on success, -1 on error
 * 
 * @error EMSGSIZE  Frame exceeds MTU
 * @error ENOBUFS   Kernel buffer full
 * 
 * PRECONDITION: Frame must have valid Ethernet header (14 bytes minimum)
 */
ssize_t tap_write(struct tap_device *dev, const uint8_t *buffer, size_t len);
```
### 4.2 Ethernet Frame Functions
```c
/**
 * Parse an Ethernet frame and extract components.
 * 
 * @param buffer         Raw frame bytes (starting with dst_mac)
 * @param len            Length of buffer
 * @param out_hdr        Output: pointer to Ethernet header (points into buffer)
 * @param out_payload    Output: pointer to payload start
 * @param out_payload_len Output: length of payload
 * @param out_vlan       Output: VLAN ID (0 if no VLAN tag)
 * @param out_ethertype  Output: Actual EtherType (after VLAN unwrapping)
 * @return               0 on success, -1 on invalid frame
 * 
 * @error EINVAL         Frame too short (< 14 bytes)
 * @error EINVAL         VLAN tag truncated
 * 
 * POSTCONDITIONS:
 * - out_hdr points into buffer at offset 0
 * - out_payload points to first byte after headers
 * - out_ethertype is in HOST byte order
 */
int eth_parse(const uint8_t *buffer, size_t len,
              struct eth_hdr **out_hdr,
              const uint8_t **out_payload,
              size_t *out_payload_len,
              uint16_t *out_vlan,
              uint16_t *out_ethertype);
/**
 * Build an Ethernet frame.
 * 
 * @param buffer     Output buffer (must be at least ETH_HDR_LEN + payload_len)
 * @param dst_mac    Destination MAC address
 * @param src_mac    Source MAC address
 * @param ethertype  EtherType value (HOST byte order, will be converted)
 * @param payload    Payload data (can be NULL)
 * @param payload_len Payload length
 * @return           Total frame length
 */
size_t eth_build(uint8_t *buffer,
                 const uint8_t *dst_mac,
                 const uint8_t *src_mac,
                 uint16_t ethertype,
                 const uint8_t *payload,
                 size_t payload_len);
/**
 * Convert MAC address to human-readable string.
 * 
 * @param mac     MAC address bytes
 * @param buf     Output buffer (must be at least 18 bytes)
 * @param buf_len Buffer length
 * @return        Pointer to buf on success, NULL on error
 * 
 * OUTPUT FORMAT: "xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx" (lowercase hex)
 */
char *mac_to_string(const uint8_t *mac, char *buf, size_t buf_len);
/**
 * Parse MAC address string into bytes.
 * 
 * @param str     Input string
 * @param mac     Output buffer (6 bytes)
 * @return        0 on success, -1 on invalid format
 * 
 * ACCEPTED FORMATS:
 * - "00:11:22:33:44:55"
 * - "00-11-22-33-44-55"
 * - "001122334455"
 */
int mac_from_string(const char *str, uint8_t *mac);
/**
 * Check if MAC address is broadcast.
 */
int mac_is_broadcast(const uint8_t *mac);
/**
 * Check if MAC address matches ours.
 */
int mac_is_ours(const uint8_t *mac, const uint8_t *our_mac);
```
### 4.3 ARP Functions
```c
/**
 * Initialize ARP cache.
 * 
 * @param cache    ARP cache to initialize
 */
void arp_cache_init(struct arp_cache *cache);
/**
 * Look up IP address in ARP cache.
 * 
 * @param cache    ARP cache
 * @param ip       IP address (host byte order)
 * @param out_mac  Output: MAC address if found
 * @return         1 if found and valid, 0 if not found/expired
 */
int arp_cache_lookup(struct arp_cache *cache, uint32_t ip, uint8_t *out_mac);
/**
 * Insert or update ARP cache entry.
 * 
 * @param cache    ARP cache
 * @param ip       IP address (host byte order)
 * @param mac      MAC address
 */
void arp_cache_insert(struct arp_cache *cache, uint32_t ip, const uint8_t *mac);
/**
 * Build an ARP request packet.
 * 
 * @param buffer    Output buffer (must be at least ARP_PKT_LEN bytes)
 * @param our_mac   Our MAC address
 * @param our_ip    Our IP address (host byte order)
 * @param target_ip IP to resolve (host byte order)
 * @return          Packet length
 */
size_t arp_build_request(uint8_t *buffer,
                         const uint8_t *our_mac,
                         uint32_t our_ip,
                         uint32_t target_ip);
/**
 * Build an ARP reply packet.
 * 
 * @param buffer     Output buffer
 * @param our_mac    Our MAC address
 * @param our_ip     Our IP address (host byte order)
 * @param target_mac MAC to reply to
 * @param target_ip  IP to reply to (host byte order)
 * @return           Packet length
 */
size_t arp_build_reply(uint8_t *buffer,
                       const uint8_t *our_mac,
                       uint32_t our_ip,
                       const uint8_t *target_mac,
                       uint32_t target_ip);
/**
 * Handle incoming ARP packet.
 * 
 * @param tap_fd    TAP device for sending responses
 * @param cache     ARP cache
 * @param pending   Pending packet queues
 * @param eth       Ethernet header of incoming frame
 * @param payload   ARP packet data
 * @param payload_len ARP packet length
 * @param our_mac   Our MAC address
 * @param our_ip    Our IP address (host byte order)
 */
void arp_handle_packet(int tap_fd,
                       struct arp_cache *cache,
                       struct pending_queues *pending,
                       struct eth_hdr *eth,
                       const uint8_t *payload,
                       size_t payload_len,
                       const uint8_t *our_mac,
                       uint32_t our_ip);
/**
 * Resolve IP to MAC, sending ARP request if needed.
 * 
 * @param tap_fd    TAP device
 * @param cache     ARP cache
 * @param pending   Pending packet queues
 * @param target_ip IP to resolve (host byte order)
 * @param our_mac   Our MAC address
 * @param our_ip    Our IP address (host byte order)
 * @param out_mac   Output: resolved MAC
 * @return          1 if resolved, 0 if pending, -1 on error
 */
int arp_resolve(int tap_fd,
                struct arp_cache *cache,
                struct pending_queues *pending,
                uint32_t target_ip,
                const uint8_t *our_mac,
                uint32_t our_ip,
                uint8_t *out_mac);
/**
 * Queue packet for later delivery (pending ARP resolution).
 * 
 * @param pending   Pending queues
 * @param ip        Target IP
 * @param data      Packet data (Ethernet frame)
 * @param len       Packet length
 * @return          0 on success, -1 if queue full
 */
int pending_queue_add(struct pending_queues *pending, uint32_t ip,
                      const uint8_t *data, size_t len);
/**
 * Initialize pending queues.
 */
void pending_queues_init(struct pending_queues *pending);
```
